content.text: switch Statements and Lookup Maps for Many Branches
When a component needs to render one of three or more distinct elements based on a single value, inline ternaries become unreadable. Two structured alternatives handle this cleanly: the
statement (imperative, paired with an element variable) and the object lookup map (declarative, defined once, indexed at render time).

attrs.alt: Two panels: switch statement approach with element variable (case blocks for each status) versus object lookup map approach (object outside component, indexed with ?? fallback)
attrs.title: switch vs Lookup Map

content.text: Consider a notification component that must render one of five different layouts based on type. Nested ternaries quickly become unmaintainable:
Four levels of indentation for five branches. Adding a sixth type means counting levels and inserting a new
pair. This is the point where
or a lookup map becomes the right tool.
statement cannot appear inside JSX directly because JSX only accepts expressions, not statements. The standard solution is to assign the result to an element variable above the return statement:
The return block stays flat and constant. Adding a new notification type is a matter of adding one new
case provides a guaranteed fallback so the component never renders nothing unexpectedly.

causes fall-through: JavaScript continues executing the next case block even when the condition does not match. Element variable assignments overwrite each other, so the rendered output comes from the wrong branch.
A lookup map is a plain JavaScript object (or

) where keys are the possible values and each value is the corresponding JSX element or component. You define it once outside the component and look up the correct value at render time by indexing with the condition:
stores factory functions rather than JSX elements directly, each call creates a fresh element with the current
prop. This is the correct approach whenever the element needs dynamic props.
When each branch renders an element with no dynamic data, you can store JSX elements directly in the map. When props vary, store factory functions or component references:
Storing component references (not JSX) is the most flexible form — React instantiates the component each render with whatever props you pass, making it easy to forward additional props.
Map Outside vs Inside the Component
Define the lookup map outside the component whenever its values do not depend on props or state. This means it is created once when the module loads, not recreated on every render:
Using TypeScript to Exhaustively Check Branches
TypeScript can verify that a lookup map covers every value of a union type. If a new status type is added but the map is not updated, the compiler flags the missing entry:
, TypeScript enforces exhaustive coverage at compile time. Every new variant must be added to the map before the code compiles — no runtime surprises.
Choosing Between switch and Lookup Map
Natural — each case block has its own statements
Yes — build from arrays or API config
One major advantage of the lookup map pattern is that it can be built programmatically from data rather than written by hand:
The configuration defines the data. The lookup map is derived. Adding a new plan tier requires only a new entry in
case or extra line in the map needed.
Some developers use an immediately-invoked function expression (IIFE) to embed a
directly inside JSX without a separate element variable. It works but makes the return block harder to scan — use sparingly:
A multi-step form wizard is a practical setting for both patterns. The active step component changes based on a step index:
Adding a new step means adding one entry to
Components with many branches benefit from parameterised tests. Instead of writing one
to keep the test file concise:
to code-split large page components. Each route only loads its bundle when the user actually navigates to it:

The lookup map here plays two roles: it defines which routes exist (any key not in the map renders a 404) and it maps each route to its code-split component. Adding a new route is one line in the map object.
When the lookup key is absent from the map, the indexed value is
. You need a fallback strategy to avoid rendering nothing or crashing. Three common approaches:
fallback form for its brevity. Use an explicit
key in the map when the fallback is a first-class rendering state (not just an error guard).
feature that lets multiple keys share the same branch. This is useful when two or more values should produce identical output:
For simple key-to-value mappings, the lookup map form is more compact. The
form with fall-through makes the intent of "these values are treated identically" more explicit.
Rendering Tables and Configuration from Maps
The lookup map pattern generalises to entire configuration objects rendered as UI. When a feature is driven by config data, you can render the whole UI by iterating the map:
The map doubles as both a component registry and a data source. Adding a new permission requires one new entry in
statement with an element variable above the return when each branch needs its own computation or multi-line logic.
Use an object lookup map when the mapping is declarative — a key maps directly to a component or element with no per-branch logic.
Store static JSX or component references in the map; store factory functions when props vary per render.
Define lookup maps outside the component to avoid recreating them on every render.
in TypeScript to get exhaustiveness checking for free.
